title: Random Variables & Expectation
subtitle: Discrete random variables, linearity, and indicator tricks
intro: A random variable is a numerical summary of a random outcome. Instead of tracking every detail of an experiment, you focus on a number like 'how many sixes' or 'total profit.' For discrete random variables, a probability mass function (PMF) lists P(X = k) for each possible value k.

The expected value E[X] is the probability-weighted average of X — your long-run center of mass. The most powerful fact for quant interviews is linearity of expectation: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] always, even when X and Y are dependent. This lets you break messy problems into simple pieces.

Indicator variables are the secret weapon. Let I_A = 1 if event A occurs and 0 otherwise. Then E[I_A] = P(A). By writing counts as sums of indicators, you can compute expectations without finding the full distribution — a technique that shows up constantly in interview problems about cards, dice, and trading scenarios.

Random variables turn stories into numbers you can average, and linearity of expectation is the most under-rated interview weapon: it holds even when variables are dependent. Stat 110 drills this through indicator tricks and named distributions—Geometric waiting times, Binomial counts, fixed-point expectations in permutations. When a Green Book puzzle asks 'how many on average,' resist building the full PMF; decompose into indicators or apply E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] first.

Random variables turn stories into numbers you can average, and linearity of expectation is the most under-rated interview weapon: it holds even when variables are dependent. Stat 110 drills this through indicator tricks and named distributions—Geometric waiting times, Binomial counts, fixed-point expectations in permutations. When a Green Book puzzle asks 'how many on average,' resist building the full PMF; decompose into indicators or apply E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] first.

questions.question: You roll a fair die and let X be the number showing. Which statement best describes X?
options: X is a discrete random variable with possible values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
X is a continuous random variable on [1, 6]
X is not random because the die is fair

explanations.A: Correct. X takes a finite set of values with assigned probabilities — that is the definition of discrete.
explanations.B: Continuous variables take uncountably many values (like exact real numbers); die faces are discrete.
explanations.C: 3.5 is the expected value, not the outcome of any single roll.
explanations.D: Fairness means equal probabilities, not determinism; X still varies randomly.

remediation.question: Let Y = 1 if a coin flip is heads and Y = 0 if tails. Y is:

explanations.A: Correct. Y takes values in {0, 1} with probabilities — discrete.
explanations.B: Y does not take arbitrary real values between 0 and 1.
explanations.C: 0.5 is E[Y] for a fair coin, not the value of Y itself.
explanations.D: Any numerical function of a random outcome is a random variable.

remediation.question: Let S be the sum of two dice. S is a discrete random variable because:
options: S takes integer values from 2 to 12 with a PMF
S can be any real number

explanations.A: Correct. S has finitely many integer outcomes each with a probability.
explanations.B: 7 is the most likely sum, but S can be 2 through 12.
explanations.C: Only integers 2–12 are possible; not all reals.
explanations.D: Functions of random outcomes are random variables.

questions.question: Which set of probabilities could be the PMF of a discrete random variable X taking values 0, 1, 2?

explanations.A: Correct. All probabilities are between 0 and 1 and sum to 1.
explanations.B: These sum to 1.1, which violates the PMF requirement.
explanations.C: Negative probabilities are impossible.
explanations.D: These sum to 1.5, which is invalid.

remediation.question: A valid PMF must satisfy:
options: each probability ≥ 0 and they sum to 1
the sum can be any positive number

explanations.A: Correct. Nonnegativity and total mass 1 are the two axioms for a PMF.
explanations.B: Equal 0.5 for every value is rarely valid and usually does not sum to 1.
explanations.C: Probabilities must be nonnegative.
explanations.D: The probabilities must sum to exactly 1.

remediation.question: If X takes values 1 and 2 with P(X=1) = p, then P(X=2) must equal:

explanations.A: Correct. The two probabilities must sum to 1.
explanations.B: P(X=2) = p would imply P(X=1) + P(X=2) = 2p, valid only if p = 0.5 and no other values.
explanations.C: 1 + p exceeds 1 for any p > 0.
explanations.D: p/2 does not follow from the sum-to-1 rule.

questions.question: A discrete random variable X has P(X=1)=0.3, P(X=2)=0.5, P(X=3)=0.2. What is E[X]?

explanations.B: 2.0 might come from rounding or averaging values without weights.
explanations.C: 1.5 is the unweighted average of 1, 2, 3, ignoring probabilities.
explanations.D: 2.5 exceeds the largest possible value of X.

remediation.question: If P(X=0)=0.6 and P(X=10)=0.4, then E[X] equals:

explanations.B: 10 is a possible value, not the expectation.
explanations.C: 6 might confuse P(X=0) with a contribution to the sum.
explanations.D: 0.4 is P(X=10), not E[X].

remediation.question: E[X] is computed by:
options: summing each value times its probability
always taking the midpoint of the range
dividing by the number of outcomes only

explanations.A: Correct. E[X] = Σ k · P(X = k).
explanations.B: The mode is not generally the expectation.
explanations.C: Midpoint ignores the actual probability distribution.
explanations.D: You must weight by probabilities, not just count outcomes.

questions.question: Which identity always holds for any random variables X and Y (even if dependent)?

explanations.A: Correct. Linearity of expectation does not require independence.
explanations.B: E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] only when X and Y are independent (with finite means).
explanations.C: Expectation of a sum is a sum of expectations, not a product.
explanations.D: There is no general rule dividing expectations for a difference.

remediation.question: If E[X] = 3 and E[Y] = −1, then E[X + Y] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. 3 + (−1) = 2 by linearity.
explanations.B: 4 might come from adding absolute values incorrectly.
explanations.C: −3 would multiply instead of add.
explanations.D: 3 ignores Y entirely.

remediation.question: Linearity of expectation requires:
options: X and Y to be independent
X and Y to be equal
X and Y to have variance 0

explanations.A: Correct. Linearity holds regardless of dependence.
explanations.B: Independence is sufficient for products, not needed for sums.
explanations.C: X and Y need not be equal.
explanations.D: Zero variance is not required.

explanations.B: 12 is 3E[X] but forgets to subtract 2.
explanations.C: 6 might come from 3 + E[X] − 1 or other incorrect arithmetic.
explanations.D: 4 is E[X] alone, ignoring the transformation.

remediation.question: If E[X] = 7, then E[−2X + 5] equals:

explanations.B: 9 ignores the negative sign on the scaling factor.
explanations.C: 19 adds instead of scaling first.
explanations.D: −14 is −2E[X] without adding 5.

remediation.question: For constants a and b, E[aX + b] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Constants pull out of expectation and shifts add directly.
explanations.B: The scaling factor a must multiply E[X].
explanations.C: The constant b must be added after scaling.
explanations.D: abE[X] would multiply constants incorrectly.

questions.question: Let I_A be the indicator of event A: I_A = 1 if A occurs, 0 otherwise. Which is true?

explanations.A: Correct. E[I_A] = (1)P(A) + (0)P(Aᶜ) = P(A).
explanations.B: 1 − P(A) is P(Aᶜ), the probability A does not occur.
explanations.C: P(A)² would arise from squaring the indicator incorrectly.
explanations.D: I_A is 1 with probability P(A), so the expectation is positive unless A is impossible.

remediation.question: If P(A) = 0.35, then E[I_A] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. The indicator expectation equals the event probability.
explanations.B: 0.65 is P(Aᶜ), not E[I_A].
explanations.C: 1 would mean A occurs with certainty.
explanations.D: 0 would mean A is impossible.

remediation.question: The indicator I_A takes the value 1 with probability:

explanations.A: Correct. By definition, I_A = 1 exactly when A happens.
explanations.B: P(Aᶜ) is the probability I_A = 0.
explanations.C: E[X] is unrelated unless X is specifically I_A.
explanations.D: I_A is not always 1 unless A is certain.

questions.question: You roll a fair die 4 times. Let N be the total number of sixes. Writing N as a sum of indicators, E[N] equals:

explanations.A: 1/6 is the expected count from a single roll, not four.
explanations.B: Correct. N = I₁ + I₂ + I₃ + I₄ with each E[Iⱼ] = 1/6, so E[N] = 4/6 = 2/3.
explanations.C: 1/3 might come from 2/6 or halving the correct answer incorrectly.
explanations.D: 4 would mean a six on every roll with certainty.

remediation.question: Flip a fair coin 10 times. Let H be the number of heads. E[H] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Each flip contributes E[I] = 0.5; 10 × 0.5 = 5.
explanations.B: 10 would require heads on every flip.
explanations.C: 0.5 is the expectation per flip, not over 10 flips.
explanations.D: 2.5 might halve the correct answer incorrectly.

remediation.question: Roll a fair die once. Let I be the indicator of rolling a 6. E[I] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. E[I] = P(6) = 1/6.
explanations.B: 1 would mean a six is certain.
explanations.C: 0 would mean a six is impossible.
explanations.D: 6 is a face value, not a probability.

questions.question: Let X be the result of one fair die roll and Y = X (same roll). X and Y are perfectly dependent, yet E[X + Y] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7 = 2E[X], even though X = Y.
explanations.B: E[X]² is not related to E[X + Y] in general.
explanations.C: E[X + Y] doubles the single-roll expectation because Y duplicates X.
explanations.D: 2 is far too small; E[X] alone is 3.5.

remediation.question: If X and Y have the same distribution and E[X] = μ, then E[X + Y] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] = μ + μ = 2μ regardless of dependence.
explanations.B: μ would ignore the contribution from Y.
explanations.C: μ² is not the expectation of a sum.
explanations.D: 0 would require negative cancellation, not generally true.

remediation.question: Linearity fails for which operation in general (without independence)?

explanations.A: Correct. E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] requires independence (or other special structure).
explanations.B: E[X + Y] always linearizes.
explanations.C: Adding a constant always works: E[X + c] = E[X] + c.
explanations.D: E[−X] = −E[X] always holds.

questions.question: A random permutation of {1, 2, …, n} is equally likely. Let F be the number of fixed points (positions where the value equals the index). Using indicators, E[F] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. F = Σ I_j with E[I_j] = P(position j fixed) = 1/n; sum over n positions gives n × (1/n) = 1.
explanations.B: n would mean every position is fixed with certainty.
explanations.C: 1/n is the probability a single position is fixed, not the total expected count.
explanations.D: n/2 has no basis here; the indicator trick gives exactly 1 for any n ≥ 1.

remediation.question: In the fixed-point problem, P(position 1 is fixed in a random permutation of n items) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Exactly one of n items lands in position 1.
explanations.B: 1 would mean position 1 is always fixed.
explanations.C: 1/2 is not the correct symmetry argument for one position.
explanations.D: Fixed points occur with positive probability.

remediation.question: If N = I₁ + I₂ + … + I_k where each E[I_j] = p, then E[N] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Linearity gives the sum of individual expectations: k × p.
explanations.B: p is the expectation of a single indicator, not the total.
explanations.C: p/k would divide instead of multiply.
explanations.D: k + p adds unrelated quantities.

questions.question: Interview classic: flip a fair coin until the first heads. Let T be the number of flips (including the first heads). E[T] equals:

explanations.A: 1 would mean heads always on the first flip.
explanations.B: Correct. T is Geometric(p=1/2) with E[T] = 1/p = 2.
explanations.C: 4 might confuse variance or another distribution.
explanations.D: 0.5 is p itself, not the expected waiting time.

remediation.question: For a Geometric(p) waiting-time variable W (counting trials until first success), E[W] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. The expected waiting time scales inversely with success probability.
explanations.B: p is the success probability, not the mean waiting time.
explanations.C: 1 − p is the failure probability on each trial.
explanations.D: p² has no standard role as the mean of W.

remediation.question: With p = 1/4, the expected number of trials until the first success is:

explanations.B: 1/4 is p, not 1/p.
explanations.C: 1 would correspond to p = 1 (certain success).
explanations.D: 2 would correspond to p = 1/2.

questions.question: A biased coin has P(heads) = 1/5. You flip until the first heads (counting the successful flip). Let T be the number of flips. What is E[T]?

explanations.A: Correct. T is Geometric(p = 1/5), so E[T] = 1/p = 5.
explanations.B: 1/5 is the success probability p, not the expected waiting time.
explanations.C: 4 might confuse E[T] with the expected number of failures before success ( (1−p)/p ).
explanations.D: 1 would mean success is guaranteed on the first flip.

remediation.question: For a Geometric(p) waiting-time variable W (trials until first success), E[W] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Expected waiting time scales inversely with success probability.
explanations.B: p is the per-trial success probability, not the mean wait.
explanations.C: 1 − p is the per-trial failure probability.
explanations.D: p² has no standard role as the mean of W.

remediation.question: With p = 1/3, the expected number of flips until the first heads is:

explanations.B: 1/3 is p itself, not the expectation.
explanations.C: 2 might come from 1/p − 1, the expected failures only.
explanations.D: 6 has no basis in the geometric mean formula.

questions.question: A trader makes 8 independent bets, each winning with probability 0.55. Let W be the number of wins. What is E[W]?

explanations.A: Correct. W ~ Binomial(n=8, p=0.55), so E[W] = np = 8(0.55) = 4.4.
explanations.B: 8 is the number of trials, not the expected count of successes.
explanations.C: 0.55 is p, the per-trial win probability, not E[W].
explanations.D: 3.2 equals n(1−p) = 8(0.45), the expected number of losses, not wins.

remediation.question: If X ~ Binomial(n, p), then E[X] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Each of n independent Bernoulli(p) trials contributes p in expectation.
explanations.B: np(1−p) is Var(X) for a binomial, not the mean.
explanations.C: p/n has no standard interpretation as a binomial mean.
explanations.D: n/p resembles a geometric mean, not a binomial count.

remediation.question: Ten fair coin flips. Expected number of heads equals:

explanations.A: Correct. E = np = 10(0.5) = 5.
explanations.B: 10 is the number of flips, not the expected head count.
explanations.C: 0.5 is p for a fair coin, not E[X].
explanations.D: 2.5 might halve the correct answer incorrectly.

placementQuestions.question: Which is a discrete random variable?
options: Number of heads in 5 coin flips
Exact height of a randomly chosen person
Time until the next bus (exact real seconds)

explanations.A: Correct. Counts take integer values 0 through 5.
explanations.B: Height measured to arbitrary precision is typically modeled as continuous.
explanations.C: Continuous time in seconds is a continuous model.
explanations.D: A constant is technically discrete but not random; the head count is the best answer.

placementQuestions.question: X takes values 10 and 20 with equal probability. E[X] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. (10 + 20)/2 = 15 with equal weights 0.5 each.
explanations.B: 10 is one possible value, not the average.
explanations.C: 20 is one possible value, not the average.
explanations.D: 30 is the sum of values, not the expectation.

placementQuestions.question: If E[X]=2 and E[Y]=5, then E[X+Y] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. 2 + 5 = 7 by linearity.
explanations.B: 10 multiplies instead of adds.
explanations.C: 3 subtracts instead of adds.
explanations.D: 2.5 averages the expectations incorrectly.

explanations.A: Correct. Indicator expectation equals event probability.
explanations.B: 0.8 is P(Aᶜ).
explanations.C: 1 would mean A is certain.
explanations.D: 0 would mean A is impossible.

explanations.B: 12 is 2E[X] without adding 3.
explanations.C: 9 might add 2+3 to E[X] incorrectly.
explanations.D: 6 is E[X] before scaling.

placementQuestions.question: Roll a fair die 3 times. Expected number of sixes equals:

explanations.B: 1/6 is the expectation per roll, not three rolls.
explanations.C: 3 would require a six every time.
explanations.D: 18 has no probabilistic meaning here.

placementQuestions.question: A PMF must have probabilities that:

explanations.A: Correct. These are the two defining properties.
explanations.B: Equal probabilities are not required.
explanations.C: Probabilities summing to 0 would mean no outcome occurs.
explanations.D: Negative or unbounded values are invalid.

placementQuestions.question: Flip a coin with P(heads)=0.25 until the first heads. Expected number of flips equals:

explanations.A: Correct. E[T] = 1/0.25 = 4.
explanations.B: 0.25 is p, not 1/p.
explanations.C: 1 would mean success is guaranteed on the first flip.
explanations.D: 2 corresponds to p = 0.5.
